annotations.label: Sampling

anchor.snippet: sampling is the selection of a subset of individuals from within a statistical population

annotations.note: Survey-statistics notion of sampling has no formalization in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Acceptance sampling

anchor.snippet: Acceptance sampling is used to determine if a production lot of material meets the governing specifications

annotations.note: Quality-control acceptance sampling is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Population

anchor.snippet: A population can be defined as including all people or items with the characteristics one wishes to understand

annotations.note: Statistical population is not a formalized concept in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Batch as population

anchor.snippet: the batch is the population

annotations.note: An illustrative example with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Roulette wheel population

anchor.snippet: Joseph Jagger studied the behaviour of roulette wheels at a casino in Monte Carlo

annotations.note: A historical anecdote with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Superpopulation

anchor.snippet: sampling theory may treat the observed population as a sample from a larger 'superpopulation'

annotations.note: The superpopulation modeling concept is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sampling frame

anchor.snippet: we seek a sampling frame which has the property that we can identify every single element

annotations.note: The survey sampling-frame concept has no formalization in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Probability sample

anchor.snippet: A probability sample is a sample in which every unit in the population has a chance

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes general probability distributions (PMF) but not the survey notion of a probability sample.

annotations.label: Estimating total street income

anchor.snippet: We want to estimate the total income of adults living in a given street

annotations.note: An illustrative estimation example with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Equal probability of selection (EPS) design

anchor.snippet: this is known as an 'equal probability of selection' (EPS) design

annotations.note: Mathlib's uniform PMF gives every element equal probability, the underlying math of EPS, but not the survey-design concept itself.

annotations.label: Common traits of probability sampling

anchor.snippet: Every element has a known nonzero probability of being sampled and involves random selection at some point

annotations.note: This characterization of probability sampling is not stated in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Nonprobability sampling

anchor.snippet: Nonprobability sampling is any sampling method where some elements of the population have no chance of selection

annotations.note: Nonprobability sampling is not a formalized concept in Mathlib.

annotations.label: First to answer the door

anchor.snippet: We visit every household in a given street, and interview the first person to answer the door

annotations.note: An illustrative example with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Simple random sample (SRS)

anchor.snippet: In a simple random sample (SRS) of a given size, all subsets of a sampling frame have an equal probability of being selected

annotations.note: Uniform PMF infrastructure can express equiprobable selection over a finset of subsets, but SRS is not defined as such in Mathlib.

annotations.label: SRS vulnerability to sampling error

anchor.snippet: Simple random sampling can be vulnerable to sampling error because the randomness of the selection

annotations.note: This qualitative claim about SRS is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Systematic sampling

anchor.snippet: Systematic sampling (also known as interval sampling) relies on arranging the study population according to some ordering scheme

annotations.note: Systematic/interval sampling has no formalization in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Systematic sampling is probability sampling

anchor.snippet: systematic sampling is a type of probability sampling

annotations.note: This classification claim is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sampling along a long street

anchor.snippet: suppose we wish to sample people from a long street that starts in a poor area

annotations.note: An illustrative example with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Vulnerability to periodicities

anchor.snippet: systematic sampling is especially vulnerable to periodicities in the list

annotations.note: This qualitative caution is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Systematic sampling is an EPS method

anchor.snippet: systematic sampling is an EPS method, because all elements have the same probability of selection

annotations.note: This property of systematic sampling is not stated in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Stratified sampling / strata

anchor.snippet: the frame can be organized by these categories into separate

annotations.note: Stratified sampling and strata are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sampling fraction

anchor.snippet: to the size of the population is called a sampling fraction

annotations.note: The sampling-fraction ratio is not a named concept in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Poststratification

anchor.snippet: Stratification is sometimes introduced after the sampling phase in a process called

annotations.note: Poststratification is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Choice-based sampling / oversampling

anchor.snippet: Choice-based sampling or oversampling is one of the stratified sampling strategies

annotations.note: Choice-based/oversampling strategies have no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Probability-proportional-to-size sampling

anchor.snippet: probability proportional to size ('PPS') sampling, in which the selection probability for each element is set to be proportional to its size measure

annotations.note: PPS sampling is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: PPS sample of six schools

anchor.snippet: Suppose we have six schools with populations of 150, 180, 200, 220, 260, and 490 students

annotations.note: A worked numerical example with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Cluster sampling

anchor.snippet: it is more cost-effective to select respondents in groups

annotations.note: Cluster sampling is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Multistage sampling

anchor.snippet: Cluster sampling is commonly implemented as multistage sampling

annotations.note: Multistage sampling has no formalization in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Quota sampling

anchor.snippet: In quota sampling , the population is first segmented into mutually exclusive sub-groups

annotations.note: Quota sampling is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sample 200 females and 300 males

anchor.snippet: an interviewer may be told to sample 200 females and 300 males between the age of 45 and 60

annotations.note: An illustrative example with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Minimax sampling

anchor.snippet: one can resample the dataset in a conservative manner called minimax sampling

annotations.note: Minimax resampling for imbalanced data is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Anderson minimax ratio equals 0.5

anchor.snippet: Anderson minimax ratio whose value is proved to be 0.5

annotations.note: This specific statistics result is absent from Mathlib (the unrelated game-theory minimax theorem does not formalize it).

annotations.label: Accidental sampling

anchor.snippet: Accidental sampling (sometimes known as grab , convenience , or opportunity sampling ) is a type of nonprobability sampling

annotations.note: Accidental/convenience sampling has no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Voluntary sampling

anchor.snippet: The voluntary sampling method is a type of nonprobability sampling

annotations.note: Voluntary sampling is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Line-intercept sampling

anchor.snippet: Line-intercept sampling is a method of sampling elements in a region whereby an element is sampled if a chosen line segment

annotations.note: Line-intercept sampling is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Panel sampling

anchor.snippet: Panel sampling is the method of first selecting a group of participants through a random sampling method

annotations.note: Panel sampling is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Snowball sampling

anchor.snippet: Snowball sampling involves finding a small group of initial respondents and using them to recruit more respondents

annotations.note: Snowball sampling is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Theoretical sampling

anchor.snippet: occurs when samples are selected on the basis of the results of the data collected so far

annotations.note: Theoretical sampling is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Active sampling

anchor.snippet: In active sampling , the samples which are used for training a machine learning algorithm are actively selected

annotations.note: Active (machine-learning) sampling is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Judgement sampling

anchor.snippet: Judgement sampling, also known as expert or purposive sampling, is a type of non-random sampling

annotations.note: Judgement/purposive sampling is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Haphazard sampling

anchor.snippet: Haphazard sampling refers to the idea of using human judgement to simulate randomness

annotations.note: Haphazard sampling is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: With/without replacement

anchor.snippet: Sampling schemes may be without replacement ('WOR' – no element can be selected more than once in the same sample) or with replacement

annotations.note: Mathlib has no sampling-with/without-replacement distribution (the only hypergeometric file is the analytic ₂F₁ function, unrelated).

annotations.label: Catch-and-release fish sampling

anchor.snippet: if we catch fish, measure them, and immediately return them to the water

annotations.note: An illustrative example with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Sampling Twitter data

anchor.snippet: there are about 600 million tweets produced every day

annotations.note: An application example with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Classification of total errors

anchor.snippet: Total errors can be classified into sampling errors and non-sampling errors

annotations.note: This survey-error taxonomy is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sampling errors and biases

anchor.snippet: Sampling errors and biases are induced by the sample design

annotations.note: Survey sampling errors and biases are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Non-sampling error

anchor.snippet: Non-sampling errors are other errors which can impact final survey estimates

annotations.note: Non-sampling error is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: When to weight data

anchor.snippet: data should usually be weighted if the sample design does not give each individual an equal chance of being selected

annotations.note: This survey-weighting guidance is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Stratified UK sample weighting

anchor.snippet: a simple random sample of individuals in the United Kingdom might not include some in remote Scottish islands

annotations.note: An illustrative weighting example with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Census

anchor.snippet: a census recording data from the entire population

annotations.note: Census as a complete enumeration is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Statistical sample

anchor.snippet: The subset, called a statistical sample (or sample , for short), is meant to reflect the whole population

annotations.note: The survey-statistics notion of a statistical sample is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Laplace's 1786 ratio-estimator population estimate

anchor.snippet: In 1786, Pierre Simon Laplace estimated the population of France by using a sample, along with ratio estimator

annotations.note: A historical illustration of Bayesian sampling inference; no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Ratio estimator

anchor.snippet: estimated the population of France by using a sample, along with ratio estimator

annotations.note: The ratio estimator from survey sampling is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Confidence interval

anchor.snippet: These were not expressed as modern confidence intervals but as the sample size

annotations.note: Confidence intervals are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Margin of error

anchor.snippet: yield a fairly accurate indicative result with a 4% margin of error at a 95% confidence interval

annotations.note: Margin of error in survey statistics is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Selection bias

anchor.snippet: Selection bias : When the true selection probabilities differ from those assumed in calculating the results

annotations.note: Selection bias as a survey-error concept is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Random sampling error

anchor.snippet: Random sampling error : Random variation in the results due to the elements in the sample being selected at random

annotations.note: Random sampling error as a survey-statistics concept is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Non-response (unit/item)

anchor.snippet: Two major types of non-response exist

annotations.note: Unit and item non-response are survey-design notions not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Survey weights

anchor.snippet: In many situations, the sample fraction may be varied by stratum and data will have to be weighted to correctly represent the population

annotations.note: Survey weights as a design-adjustment device are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Poisson sampling

anchor.snippet: these selection probabilities can then be used as the basis for Poisson sampling

annotations.note: Poisson sampling (independent Bernoulli inclusions per element) is not formalized as a survey-sampling scheme in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sample size determination

anchor.snippet: Formulas, tables, and power function charts are well known approaches to determine sample size

annotations.note: Sample size determination via power/effect-size formulas is not formalized in Mathlib.
